revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Jogu-jinja]][[ja:常宮神社]] {{Expand Japanese|常宮神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Jogu Shrine | native_name = 常宮神社 | image = Jogu-jinja haisho.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Jogu Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.69036111111111|136.0296111111111}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Jogu Shrine'''|常宮神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Echizen Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} * {{ill|Q135160342|ltq=Q135160342|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database * {{ill|Q135009906|ltq=Q135009906|qid=Q135009906|1=Massha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Supershrine|lt=Shikinai Supershrine|qid=Q135419779|1=Shikinai Supershrine}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11636413|ltq=Q11636413|qid=Q11636413|1=List of Shikinaisha in Echizen Province}} ** Series ordinal: 29 ** Series ordinal: 33 ** Series ordinal: 34 ** P155: {{ill|Q135040141|ltq=Q135040141|qid=Q135040141|1=Amekunihimeno Shrine}} ** P155: {{ill|Jogu Shrine|lt=Jogu Shrine|ja|常宮神社|qid=Q11481363|1=Jogu Shrine}} ** P155: {{ill|Q135040138|ltq=Q135040138|qid=Q135040138|1=Ihatano Shrine}} ** P155: {{ill|Q135098992|ltq=Q135098992|qid=Q135098992|1=Isanahikono Shrine}} ** P155: {{ill|Amekunitsuhikono Shrine|lt=Amekunitsuhikono Shrine|qid=Q135040140|1=Amekunitsuhikono Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q11481363|ltq=Q11481363|qid=Q11481363|1=Jogu Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q135040142|ltq=Q135040142|qid=Q135040142|1=Shirokihikono Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Ametotsurukino Shrine|lt=Ametotsurukino Shrine|qid=Q135098990|1=Ametotsurukino Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q135040141|ltq=Q135040141|qid=Q135040141|1=Amekunihimeno Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q135098994|ltq=Q135098994|qid=Q135098994|1=Amesusuno Shrine}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Echizen Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Echizen Province|ja|越前国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11636413|1=List of Shikinaisha in Echizen Province}} ** Series ordinal: 29 ** P155: {{ill|Q135040138|ltq=Q135040138|qid=Q135040138|1=Ihatano Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Ametotsurukino Shrine|lt=Ametotsurukino Shrine|qid=Q135098990|1=Ametotsurukino Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q847581|ltq=Q847581|qid=Q847581|1=Tsuruga, Fukui}} * {{ill|Echizen Province|lt=Echizen Province|ceb|Echizen|cs|Provincie Ečizen|de|Provinz Echizen|en|Echizen Province|es|Provincia de Echizen|fa|ولایت اچیزن|fr|Province d'Echizen|gl|Provincia de Echizen|id|Provinsi Echizen|it|Echizen (provincia)|ja|越前国|ko|에치젠국|lt|Ečidzeno žemė|nl|Echizen (provincie)|pt|Província de Echizen|ru|Этидзэн (провинция)|simple|Echizen Province|sk|Ečizen (provincia)|sr|Провинција Ечизен|tr|Echizen (vilayet)|uk|Провінція Етідзен|zh_min_nan|Etizen-kok|zh_yue|越前國|zh|越前國|qid=Q1047144|1=Echizen Province}} * {{ill|Q11499560|ltq=Q11499560|qid=Q11499560|1=Tsuruga District, Fukui}} == coordinate location (P625) == * == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 182216 ** P3831: {{ill|Q135159299|ltq=Q135159299|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} * 182220 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} * 182221 ** P3831: {{ill|Q135159299|ltq=Q135159299|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q90139226|ltq=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|1=Ken-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == P14005 (P14005) == * {{ill|Q11071127|ltq=Q11071127|qid=Q11071127|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} * {{ill|Q11071125|ltq=Q11071125|qid=Q11071125|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * アメヤホヨロツ- == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7210005006114 == Commons category (P373) == * Jogu-jinja == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Q135040140|ltq=Q135040140|qid=Q135040140|1=Amekunitsuhikono Shrine}} * {{ill|Q135040141|ltq=Q135040141|qid=Q135040141|1=Amekunihimeno Shrine}} * {{ill|Ameyahoyorotsu- Shrine|lt=Ameyahoyorotsu- Shrine|qid=Q135040139|1=Ameyahoyorotsu- Shrine}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == has part(s) (P527) == * {{ill|Q135098994|ltq=Q135098994|qid=Q135098994|1=Amesusuno Shrine}} * {{ill|Tamasasarahikono Shrine|lt=Tamasasarahikono Shrine|qid=Q135098997|1=Tamasasarahikono Shrine}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福井県敦賀市常宮13-16', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Jogu-jinja}} [[JA:常宮神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11481363}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Tsunemiya Shrine |Image = [[File:Jogu-jinja haisho.JPG|270px]]
Place of worship (Fukui Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukui Prefecture|Q133879|qid=Q133879|1=Fukui Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tsuruga City|Q847581|qid=Q847581|1=Tsuruga, Fukui}}Jonomiya 13-16 |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ten Yaoyoromanbi Mikoto |Divinity = |Shakaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (Small) Ronsha
Formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Kehi Jingu|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|1=Kehi Shrine}}Moto Okumiya/Setsusha |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Sangensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=July 22nd|Q2725|qid=Q2725|1=July 22}} (General Festival) |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukui|label=Tonemiya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }}{{List of coordinates}} [[File:Jogu-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Jōgu Shrine'' (Jōgu Jinja) is located in Jōmiya, {{ill|lt=Fukui Prefecture|Q133879|qid=Q133879|1=Fukui Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tsuruga City|Q847581|qid=Q847581|1=Tsuruga, Fukui}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Divine Crest|1=UNKNOWN}} is "16-petal Yaegiku," "Tomoe," and "Paulownia." . In ancient times, it was the inner shrine and set shrine of {{ill|lt=Kehi Shrine|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|1=Kehi Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Echizen Province|Q1047144|qid=Q1047144|1=Echizen Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}). === Company name === Tsunenomiya Shrine was also called ``Tsunenomiya'', ``Tsunenomiya Gozen'', and ``Tsunenomiya Daigongen'' in ancient times. The name ``Jonomiya'' is said to be named after {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}}'s {{ill|lt=oracle|Q217123|qid=Q217123|1=Oracle}}, ``Always enjoy the palace, where the waves and wind are calm.''{{Sfn|Jonomiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}}. In ancient times, it was read as ``tsune (no) miya,'' but in the early modern period, it was read as ``jougu,'' and in the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ( The current name and pronunciation of the company was set in {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|qid=Q7717|1=1868}}). Also, ``{{ill|lt=Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}'' and ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}} {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1106 4932|qid=Q1106 4932|lt=Tenyaoyorozuhime Shrine (Ame no Yorozu Himejinja), which can be seen in the Shinmeicho}}, is said to be the name of the ancient Tokomiya Shrine {{Sfn|Tokomiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}} (there are also different theories).In addition, Tsunemiya Shrine had a deep relationship with {{ill|lt=Kehi Shrine|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|1=Kehi Shrine}}, so in ancient times it was also referred to as ``Hongu/Setsusha'', ``Kuchinomiya/Okumiya'', ``Himoroginomiya/Kagami no Miya'', ``Kamisha/Shimosha'', etc.{{Sfn|Kehi Shrine (Gods)|1985|p=125-127}}. === Enshrined God === The following seven deities are enshrined in the Shrine History Book. . ; Main shrine (main shrine) :* ``Amenoyorozuhime no Mikoto'' (Amenoya Oyorozuhime no Mikoto) - Commonly known as ``Tokomiya Okami''. :* {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} (Jingu Kougou) - Empress of Emperor Chuai. :* {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} - 14th Emperor. ; 4 shrines surrounding the main shrine :* East Palace: {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) :* Sojagu Shrine: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} (Oujin Tennou) - 15th Emperor. :* Tairadenomiya: Tamahime no Mikoto (Tamahime no Mikoto, Tamahime no Mikoto) - Empress Jingu's younger sister. :* West Palace: {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|qid=Q1073668|lt=Takeuchi Sukune no Mikoto|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} (Takeuchi Sukune no Mikoto)According to the shrine's legend, Ten Yaoyoroman Hime no Mikoto has been enshrined since ancient times as the god of sericulture, so it is thought that Ten Yaomo Hime no Mikoto is a local deity. The other six pillars are based on the legend of Empress Jingu going to war. (Japan)|Q1346269|Taiho}}3 ({{ill|lt=703 years|Q23500|qid=Q23500|1=703}}), it is said to have been jointly enshrined in {{Sfn|Kehi Shrine (Gods)|1985|p =125-127}}, and it is speculated that this was a combined enshrinement as the Hachiman faith spread at the time. These enshrined deities are the same as {{ill|lt=Kihi Shrine|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|1=Kehi Shrine}}, except that the main deity enshrined is Ten Yaoyoroman Hime no Mikoto (the main deity enshrined at Kehi Shrine) rather than Izabetsu no Mikoto (the main deity enshrined at Kehi Shrine). Tsunegu-jingu is said to have a close relationship with Kehi-jingu, and one theory is that the Sosansai (regular festival), which is attended by pilgrims from Kehi-jingu Shrine, is a Shinto ritual in which Izabetsu-no-Mikoto visits Tenyaomanhi-no-Mikoto.{{Sfn|Kehi-jingu Shrine (Gods)|1985|p=125-127}}.As for the deities enshrined in the main shrine, it is stated in the ``Jōmiya Honki'' and the detailed account book of {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|qid=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}}3 ({{ill|lt=1718|Q6899|qid=Q6899|1=1718}}) that there are three deities as mentioned above. However, according to another theory, in "Kehigu Shrine" (an old record of {{ill|lt=Kehi Shrine|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|1=Kehi Shrine}}), there are two deities enshrined, and the main deity is Empress Jingu, and "Ten Yaoyoromanhi Mikoto" is said to be the same. It is said to be another name for Emperor Chuai, and it is said that Emperor Chuai is enshrined in the {{ill|lt=Aiden|Q135009883|qid=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. It is said that Empress Jingu gave birth to {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} in Tsukushi while wearing a belly band at Jogu Shrine, so Tsunegu Shrine is worshiped as the deity of safe childbirth. It is also said that Empress Jingu prayed for safe passage on sea routes during the {{ill|lt=Sankan Conquest|Q11249435|qid=Q11249435|1=Three Han Campaign}}, so she is also worshiped as the guardian deity of navigation and fishing. === History === === Founding === [[File:Kehi-jingu otorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Kehi Jingu|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|1=Kehi Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Tsuruga City|Q847581|qid=Q847581|1=Tsuruga, Fukui}}Akebono Town)}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Echizen Province|Q1047144|qid=Q1047144|1=Echizen Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. It has a deep relationship with Tsunemiya Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, the main enshrined deity, Ten Yaoyoromanhiba-no-Mikoto, has resided here since ancient times. After {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}, the Emperor and {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} worshiped Kehijin in February 2010. When going on a conquest|Q11249435}}, the Emperor went alone to Nagato via Kii, but the Empress remained at the permanent palace, plotting a foreign expedition, and reported that she set out on the first day of the rabbit in June.. Then {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|qid=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=703|Q23500|qid=Q23500|1=703}}), the shrine building was repaired by imperial order, and {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} and {{jalink|Emperor Chuai|Q179971} }, {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru no Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, Tamahime no Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Sukune|Q1073668|qid=Q1073668|lt=Takeuchi Sukune no Mikoto|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} are reported to have been enshrined together.. After that, it was said to have operated as a shrine outside the precincts of Kehi Shrine.On the other hand, according to "Kehigushaki", during the reign of Emperor Kanmu, the divine spirit of Empress Jingu appeared and gave an oracle that said, "I will always stay in the palace," so the Empress was praised and enshrined as "Ten Yaoyoromanhiba Okami," and further, {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} There is a separate biography that states that he {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=solicited|1=UNKNOWN}} Empress Jingu in 11 years ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|qid=Q23835|1=844}}). Behind the shrine grounds are a group of side caves that are thought to be from the late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}} period, and because this area is an anchorage in Tsuruga Bay, it is thought that people settled there from an early time {{Sfn|Kehi Shrine (Gods)|1985|p=125-127}}. In connection with this, there is a theory that the beginning of the connection between Kehi Shrine and Tsunemiya Shrine lies in the relationship between the peacetime port (Tsurugazu) and the evacuation anchorage port (Jonomiya Bay) {{Sfn|Kehi Shrine (Gods)|1985|p=125-127}}. === Outline ===According to the national history, in {{ill|lt=Narihira|Q1324664|qid=Q1324664|1=Saikō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=856|Q30370|qid=Q30370|1=856}}), ``Tenyaoyorozu Himejin'' was listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=government shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. In the same year, the same god was enshrined as {{ill|lt=Divine Grade|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} {{Sfn|Jonomiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}}. Also {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ink|Echizen Province|Q1047144}}{{ill|lt=Tsuruga District|Q11499560|qid=Q11499560|1=Tsuruga District, Fukui}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi Shrine|1=Shikinaisha}} can be found listed as ``Tenyaoyorozu Hime Shrine''{{Sfn|Jonomiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}}. Later, in the ``Echizen Domestic Shinto Name Book,'' there is a mention of ``Junior Second Rank Ten Yaoyorozu Hiba Daimyojin.'' Tsunemiya Shrine is thought to correspond to the "Tenyaoyorozu Hime-kami" seen in these documents {{Sfn|Tsunemiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}} (However, there are different theoriesIn addition to Tsunemiya Shrine, Tenyaoyorozu Himejin Shrine, the subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Oshio Hachiman Shrine|Q11434131|qid=Q11434131|1=Ōshio Hachimangu}} in {{ill|lt=Echizen City|Q877941|qid=Q877941|1=Echizen, Fukui}}, is mentioned in the essay about "Tenyaoyorozu Himejin", but no conclusion has been reached. {{Harv|Ten Yaoyoroman Hime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=232-233}}).According to the shrine's legend, it was later rebuilt in 4 years ({{ill|lt=1015|Q19245|qid=Q19245|1=1015}}) by the priest Enshu of the {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai monk|1=Tiantai}}. According to later records, in {{ill|lt=Kyoroku|Q1193418|qid=Q1193418|1=Kyōroku}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1529|Q6421|qid=Q6421|1=1529}}), {{ill|lt=Takakage Asakura (10th head of the family)|Q11068401|qid=Q11068401|lt=Takakage Asakura|1=Asakura Takakage}} and {{ill|lt=Kagenori Asakura|Q11068680|qid=Q11068680|lt=Kagenori|1=Asakura Kagetoshi}} repaired and built the east hall {{Sfn|Jonomiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}}. Then, after the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|qid=Q6680|1=1574}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Bunroku|1=UNKNOWN}}4 ({{ill|lt=1595|Q6782|qid=Q6782|1=1595}}), {{ill|lt=Tsuruga Castle|Q11255713|qid=Q11255713|lt=Tsuruga Castle Lord|1=Tsuruga Castle}} {{jalink|Otani} Yoshitsugu|Q386265}} established a temporary shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}7 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|qid=Q6727|1=1602}}) Echizen Province The main shrine (main shrine) was built by the lord {{ill|lt=Hideyasu Yuki|Q1130929|qid=Q1130929|1=Yūki Hideyasu}}{{Sfn|Jonomiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}}. It is also said that Hideyasu donated Tsunemiyaura (33 koku 5 to) as a shrine site {{Sfn|Tonemiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}}.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}4 ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}}), it was designated as Kehi Jingu Setsusha by the Obama clan. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|qid=Q7766|1=1876}}), it was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern company ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in Meiji 10 ({{ill|lt=1877|Q7773|qid=Q7773|1=1877}}), {{ill|lt=Ministry of the Interior (Japan)|Q1142265|qid=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|1=Home Ministry}} designated it as a sub-sha of the Jingu, but since then it has become independent from the Jingu. === God level === A record of {{ill|lt=Divine rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication to ``Tenyaoyorozu Himegami'' (there are different opinions about the dedication ). * Six Kingdoms Historical Period ** On September 17, {{ill|lt=Narihira|Q1324664|qid=Q1324664|1=Saikō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=856|Q30370|qid=Q30370|1=856}}), it was listed as a government shrine (``{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Emperor's Annals|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}'') - It is written as ``Tenyaoyorozu Himakami.'' ** September 28, 856, Junior Fourth Rank (Junior Montoku Emperor Jitsuroku) - Written as ``Tenyaoyorozu Himajin.'' * After Six Kingdoms History ** Junior Second Rank (``Echizen Domestic Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Tenyaoyorozu Hima Daimyojin.'' === Shinto priest === There is no {{ill|lt=shake|Q11588367|qid=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}} at Tsunemiya Shrine, and until {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|qid=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} in the Meiji era, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine priests|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Tendai sect performed rituals {{Sfn|Tenyaoyoromanhiba Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1986|p=237}}. As for the temples of shrine priests, there are six temples that can be seen in the {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|qid=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} dated 1595: ``Hozobo'', ``Kojobo'', ``Jiyobo'', ``Jorizobo'', ``Izumizobo'', and ``Daijyobo'' {{Sfn|Ten Yaoyoromanhi Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1986|p=237}}. Furthermore, in addition to the above, "Jijubo" and "Hosenbo" are written on the bell tag of the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1661|Q7005|qid=Q7005|1=1661}}). On the ridge tag dated 5th year ({{ill|lt=1822|Q6983|qid=Q6983|1=1822}}), "Enzobo" is mentioned. The above-mentioned shrine priests returned to secular life and became Shinto priests in April of the first year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|qid=Q7717|1=1868}}), but the only one left now is the Miyamoto family, which descends from the Hozobo family. === Precincts === [[File:Jogu-jinja honden.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main shrine/place of worship
(both tangible cultural properties designated by Fukui prefecture)}}]] The precincts are 4,455 tsubo {{Sfn|Tsunemiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}} (approximately 1.5 hectares). The main shrine building is a reconstruction from the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. The main shrine was rebuilt in 1713|Q6873}} in the middle of the Edo period. Sangen-sha {{ill|lt=Nagaruzo|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} has three rooms on the front (girder row) and three rooms on the side (between the beams), with one room between the beams in the front serving as the vestibule, and a one-room prayer hall attached to the front. The roof used to be {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|qid=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}, but now it has a copper plate roof. The main body is a circular pillar, and the front chamber and facing prayer are square pillars. The front chamber is one step lower than the main chamber, and there are carvings on the rainbow beams and other areas. It has been pointed out that the decoration and form of this main hall are similar to the old main hall of Kehi Jingu Shrine (built in the early Edo period, but burnt down in an air raid), and it is also thought that it is a form in which the rear part of the shrine's Ryoryu-zukuri style was omitted. This main shrine is designated as a tangible cultural property of Fukui Prefecture. Precinct information board. The ``Place of Worship'' is a building with one room in the front and two sides on all sides (no walls or fittings), with a roof made of Karahafu style and copper plate. It was originally the middle gate (Mukakaramon) of Kehi Shrine, and was moved to its current location in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}18 ({{ill|lt=1943|Q18623|qid=Q18623|1=1943}}). {{harv|"Fukui Prefecture Shrine Journal"}} was written in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}14 ({{ill|lt=1637|Q6889|qid=Q6889|1=1637}}), the first {{ill|lt=Obama clan|Q1088442|qid=Q1088442|lt=Obama feudal lord|1=Obama Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Sakai Tadakatsu (Obama feudal lord)|Q7402768|qid=Q7402768|lt=Tadakatsu Sakai|1=Sakai Tadakatsu}}, but the detailed style suggests that it was built in the late Edo period. '''Chumon'''' was built in {{ill|lt=Horeki|Q1194397|qid=Q1194397|1=Hōreki}}12 ({{ill|lt=1762|Q7652|qid=Q7652|1=1762}}) in the middle of the Edo period. It is a four-legged gate with one room in the front and two rooms on the side, and the roof is made of copper plates. These places of worship and the middle gate, along with the main shrine, have been designated as tangible cultural properties of Fukui Prefecture. {{sfn|Tami. Yoshida (2009)|p=386-387}}Around the main shrine, there are four shrines, ``Higashidengu'''' (front east), ``Western shrine'' (front west), ``Soshagu'', and ``Tairadengu'' (both back east), similar to Kehi Jingu. Together with the main shrine, these shrines are called ``Daigoza'' {{Sfn|Ten Yaoyoromanhiba Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=234}}. Other shrine buildings include a worship hall facing the sea where Kehi Shrine is worshiped, and a treasure trove that houses the Korean Bell, a national treasure. In addition, there is a large tree called ``Ryutomatsu'' that stands along the sea's edge. It is said that a group of flames ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ryutō|1=UNKNOWN}}) rise from the sea of ​​{{ill|lt=Tsuruga Bay|Q11499550|qid=Q11499550|1=Tsuruga Bay}} and stay on this pine tree at dawn in the early hours of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=New Year's Day|1=UNKNOWN}}, and these lights are said to be offered to light the New Year's Day.{{Sfn|Jonomiya Shrine (Heibonsha)|1981|p=523-524}}. File:Jogu-jinja toudengu.JPG|East Palace File:Jogu-jinja seidengu.JPG|West Palace File:Jogu-jinja soujagu.JPG|Sojagu Shrine File:Jogu-jinja heidengu.JPG|Heidengu Palace File:Jogu-jinja houmotsu.JPG|Treasure House File:Jogu-jinja chumon.JPG|Chumon (Fukui Prefecture designated tangible cultural property) File:Jogu-jinja haiden-1.JPG|Oratory (land side) File:Jogu-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Haden of worship (sea side) === Setsusha === === 4 Shikinai companies === To the west of the main shrine are four shrines with connected roofs, each of which is considered a Shikinai shrine. ; Tengoku Tsuhiko Shrine :* Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Azumo Isora|Q1755974|qid=Q1755974|lt=Isora Okami|1=Azumi-no-isora}} :* Shrine rank: Shikinaisha "Tengaku Tsuhiko Shrine" {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}In addition to the Ronsha of "Tengaku Tsuhiko Shrine", there is also Oshio Hachimangu Shrine terminal shrine and Tendon Tsuhiko Shrine in Echizen City. {{Harv|Amokuni Tsuhiko Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=249-250}}. , Echizen Domestic Shinto Name Book "Sho Fourth Rank Tenkutsuhikojin" Ronsha{{Sfn|Amokunitsuhiko Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1986|p=249-250}} : The enshrined deity "Isora Okami" refers to {{ill|lt=Azumo Isora|Q1755974|qid=Q1755974|1=Azumi-no-isora}}, which is related to the Sankan Conquest. There is a theory that the enshrined deity is {{ill|lt=Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|Q65318|qid=Q65318|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}}'s younger brother, {{ill|lt=Wakatakehiko no Mikoto|Q11596350|qid=Q11596350|lt=Wakatake no Mikoto|1=Wakatakehiko}}, but the details are unknown. ; Tengaku Tsuhime Shrine :* Enshrined deity: Dragon Goddess:* Shrine rank: Shikiuchi shrine "Tengaku Tsuhime Shrine (Tengaku Tsuhime Shrine)" Ronsha The other shrines of "Tengaku Tsuhime Shrine" include Oshio Hachiman Shrine terminal shrine and Tengaku Tsuhime Shrine in Echizen City. {{Harv|Amokuni Tsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=253}}. , Echizen Domestic Shinto Name Book "Sho Fourth Rank Tenkutsuhime God" Ronsha{{Sfn|Amokunitsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=253}} ; Tenzu Shrine :* Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Three Gods|Q1114013|qid=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Okami|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} :* Shrine rank: Shikinaisha "Tenzu Shrine", Echizen Domestic Shinto Name Book "Sho Fourth Rank Tensujin" {{Sfn|Tenzu Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1986|p=255}} ; Ifu Shrine :* Enshrined deity: Shiraki Hikokami :* Shrine status: Shikinaisha "Tamasasara Yoshihiko Shrine", Echizen Domestic Shinto Name Book "Sho4 Rank Tentama Sasarahikojin" {{Sfn|Tamasasara Yoshihiko Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=257}} : The enshrined deity is either {{ill|lt=Kora Taisha|Q715647|qid=Q715647|lt=Kora Tadamari-myo|1=Kōra taisha}} or Tamasara Yoshihiko Shrine {{Sfn|Tamasa Yoshihiko Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=257}}. According to the "Nihon Montoku Emperor Annals," the four deities of Tenmokutsuhime, Tengokutsuhiko, Tensuzu, and Tamasara Yoshihiko, together with Tenyaoyorozuhime, are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Narihira|1=UNKNOWN}}3 ({{ill|lt=856|Q30370|qid=Q30370|1=856}}), he was entrusted to a government shrine and was given the rank of {{ill|lt=Divine Grade|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}, which is the lower rank of Junior Fifth Rank. === Others === Other Shrines within the Precincts{{Sfn|Ten Yaoyoromanhiba Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=238}}. * Chikubushima Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Ichikishima Hime|Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishima Himejin|1=Ichikishima-hime}} * Sarutahiko Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Sartahiko|Q3090037|qid=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahikojin|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}} * Shinmei Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kotaijin|1=Amaterasu}}, {{ill|lt=Toyouke Omikami|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Omikami}} * Inari Shrine - Deity enshrined: Toyotama Inari Okami * Ebisu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, Ebisu Okami File:Jogu-jinja keidaisha-1.JPG|Inari Shrine (left)/Ebisu Shrine (right) === Festival === {{ill|lt=Reigisai|Q11385469|qid=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on {{ill|lt=July 22nd|Q2725|qid=Q2725|1=July 22}} and is called ``''Sosansai'' (Sosansai/Sou no Umairi). This is a festival in which the gods of Kehi Shrine, along with the chief priest and other priests, visit Tokomiya Shrine, and it is a ritual that symbolizes the long-standing connection between Kehi Shrine and Tokomiya Shrine. During the festival, the divine spirits of the shrine are transported in a boat-shaped mikoshi, depart from Miyukihama on the barge ``Jingumaru'', and after a festival is held on the boat, they arrive at Tsunegu Shrine. Fishing is prohibited on this day, and fishermen tow the boats in small boats, believing that if they serve the barges, they will be blessed with a bountiful catch. As for its origin, there are two theories: one is based on a legend in which Empress Jingu led a military army to war, and the other is that the positive deity Kehi no Mikoto (Izabetsu no Mikoto) visits the negative deity Tsunegu no Mikoto (Ten Yaoyoromanhi no Mikoto). {{Sfn|Kehi Shrine (Gods)|1985|p=125-127}} In addition, until the early Meiji period, Shinto rituals based on the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman faith|1=UNKNOWN}}, such as {{ill|lt=Hojo-e|Q11078912|qid=Q11078912|1=Life release}}, were held {{Sfn|Ten Yaoyoroman Hima Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986|p=237}}. === Cultural assets === === National Treasure === [[File:Jogu Jinsha Bell (261).jpg|thumb|150px|{{center|Joseon Bell (National Treasure)}}]] * {{ill|lt=Korean Bell|Q11517870|qid=Q11517870|1=Korean bell}} (Craft)*: It is a large {{ill|lt=Bōshō|Q8192099|qid=Q8192099|1=Bonshō}} with a total height of 112.0 cm and a diameter of 66.7 cm, and a celestial maiden statue is cast on the body. It is a Korean bell with a different style from the Japanese bell, and was made during the {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|qid=Q28456|lt=Unified Silla|1=Silla}} period. There are inscriptions such as ``March date of the 7th year of Taiwa, Zhongcheng, Lianchi Temple, Jingzhou,'' but ``{{ill|lt=Yamato (Tang)|Q10934252|qid=Q10934252|lt=Taewa|1=Dahe Era}}7'' is the year name of the Tang Dynasty and corresponds to {{ill|lt=833|Q30377|qid=Q30377|lt=833 AD|1=833}}, and this bell is one of the few relics of Silla bells cast during the Unified Silla period. In the shrine biography, it is said that it was brought back from Korea during the {{ill|lt=Bunroku/Keichō no Eki|Q576338|qid=Q576338|lt=Bunroku no Eki|1=Imjin War}}, and in the second year of {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} ({{ill|lt=159 It is said that in 7th year|Q6790|qid=Q6790|1=1597}}, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|qid=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} dedicated {{ill|lt=Yoshitsugu Otani|Q386265|qid=Q386265|1=Ōtani Yoshitsugu}} as a messenger to Tokomiya Shrine in honor of Empress Jingu's conquest of the Three Koreas. However, there is also a theory that it was brought by {{ill|lt=Wakō|Q697605|qid=Q697605|1=Wokou}}. It was designated as a national treasure on April 7, 1908, based on the then {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|qid=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}}, and on November 22, 1952, it was designated as a national treasure based on {{ill|lt=Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502|qid=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}}. "Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia Japan's 84 National Treasures" (Asahi Shimbun, 1999), p.9-114 (commentary written by Eiichi Nomura). {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|366| Korean Bell}}
[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/kougei/jogujinja-chosensho.html Korean Bell] (Fukui Prefecture homepage). === Fukui Prefecture designated cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Main shrine, place of worship, and middle gate (3 buildings) - The main shrine was designated on April 23, 1980, and the place of worship and middle gate were additionally designated on March 31, 2009[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/kenzou/jougujinja-jinja.html Tsunemiya Shrine main hall, place of worship, middle gate] (Fukui Prefecture homepage). . === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Fukui Prefecture|Q133879|qid=Q133879|1=Fukui Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tsuruga City|Q847581|qid=Q847581|1=Tsuruga, Fukui}}Jonomiya 13-16 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway|Q502125|qid=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} (JR West) {{ill|lt=Hokuriku Main Line|Q1318747|qid=Q1318747|1=Hokuriku Main Line}}, {{ill|lt=Obama Line|Q1058227|qid=Q1058227|1=Obama Line}} {{ill|lt=Tsuruga Station|Q5364670|qid=Q5364670|1=Tsuruga Station}} ** Take the Fukutetsu Bus (Jonomiya Line bound for Tateishi) and get off at the "Jonomiya" bus stop (about 22 minutes, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Parrot stone *: A huge rock 30 meters high and 21 meters wide located halfway up {{ill|lt=Nishigatake|Q11628327|qid=Q11628327|1=Mount Saihō}} (Saihogatake) behind the shrine. It is said that when you call out to this stone, human voices and bird calls are often heard in return, so it was named ``parrot stone''. It is also called the "word stone". Since it was discovered during the {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|qid=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} years ({{ill|lt=1781|Q7719|qid=Q7719|1=1781}}-{{ill|lt=1789|Q7772|qid=Q7772|1=1789}}) and is described in detail in travelogues, it has been designated as a designated historical site by Tsuruga City as the ``Jōmiya Aum Rock''. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * Encyclopedia ** "Great Dictionary of Shinto, reduced edition" supervised by {{ill|lt=Naoichi Shoten|Q1054765|qid=Q1054765|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}|isbn=978-4040011806|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference=``Tonemiya''|ref={{Harvid|Tonemiya (Kadokawa)|1989}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=``Tonemiya Shrine''|ref={{Harvid|Tonemiya Shrine (Kadokawa)|1989}}}}. * Other books** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Tsunemiya Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103|qid=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} *** [{{NDLDC|1088278/698}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Middle"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 698-699. ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|qid=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}|author=|year=1985|chapter=|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 8 Hokuriku|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|qid=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022186|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference=Hideyuki Nishimura "Kehi Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kehi Shrine (Gods)|1985}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Hideyuki Nishimura "Jonomiya Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Jonomiya Shrine (Gods)|1985}}}}. ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikinaisha Research Group|author=|year=1986|title=Shikinaisha Research Report Volume 15|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakukan University|Q1797584|qid=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref=}}*** Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Amokunitsuhiko Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1986}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Kanichi Tanaka "Amokunitsuhiko Shrine" |ref={{Harvid|Amokunitsu Hime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Kanichi Tanaka "Tenzu Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Tenzu Shrine Shrine (Shikiuchisha) |1986}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Kanichi Tanaka "Tamasa Yoshihiko Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Tamasa Yoshihiko Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1986}}}}. ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Fukui Prefectural Shrine Agency |title=Fukui Prefectural Shrine Magazine: Godaiten Memorial |publisher=Fukui Prefectural Shrine Agency |year=1994 |edition=Not for sale |NCID=BN11906220 |ref={{harvid|``Fukui Prefectural Shrine Magazine''}}}} ***〈Original 1936〉{{Cite book|Japanese book|title=Fukui Prefecture Shrine Magazine |publisher=Fukui Prefecture Shinto Association |year=1936 |id={{National Bibliographic Number|46071333}} |doi=10.11501/1222714 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1222714 |quote=National Diet Library Digital Collection}}** {{Cite journal|Japanese book|author=Yoshito Tama, Junichi Yoshida |date=2009-08 |url=https://doi.org/10.57375/00001323 |title=Report on the architectural survey of the shrine and central gate of Tsunemiya Shrine in Tsuruga |journal=Fukui Institute of Technology Research Bulletin |ISSN=18844456 |publisher=Fukui Institute of Technology |issue=39 |pages=384-391 |doi=10.57375/00001323 |CRID=1390859238005302656 |ref={{harvid|Tami. Yoshida (2009)}}}} == Related Literature == * Kehi Shrine, Kehi Shrine, 1941. ** See [{{NDLDC|1040162/117}} "Kehigushaki"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection). === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kehi Shrine|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|1=Kehi Shrine}} (Akebono-cho, Tsuruga City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|39|17.85|N|136|4|28.92|E|region:JP-18_type:landmark|location|name=Kehi Shrine}}) * {{ill|lt=Oshio Hachimangu Shrine|Q11434131|qid=Q11434131|1=Ōshio Hachimangu}} ({{ill|lt=Echizen City|Q877941|qid=Q877941|1=Echizen, Fukui}}) - Ronsha of Shikinaisha "Tenpachi Hyakuman Hime Shrine" in the precincts. * Tsune Shrine ({{ill|lt=Mikata Kaminaka-gun|Q1204541|qid=Q1204541|1=Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1349039|qid=Q1349039|1=Wakasa, Fukui}}) - It has been pointed out that it is related to Tsunemiya Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Return issue of cultural properties leaked from the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829|qid=Q11517829|1=repatriation of cultural property to Korea}} === External link === {{Commonscat|Jogu-jinja}} * [http://www.jinja-fukui.jp/contents/list.php?act=view&id=1784 Tsunemiya Shrine] - Fukui Prefectural Shrine Agency * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/300901.html Tenyaoyorozu Hime Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection” {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Jogu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Jogu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Massha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echizen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsuruga, Fukui]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echizen Province]] [[Category:福井県の旧県社]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan (Crafts)]] [[Category:National Treasure of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tsuruga, Fukui]]
